speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Wednesday, February the 28th, 1996. The survivor is Freda Wineman. Her last name at birth was Silberberg. The interviewer is Sharon Tyler. The city and state is London, England. And the language of the interview is English. Today's date is Wednesday, February the 28th, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Freda Wineman. We're in London, England. Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Freda Wineman. W-I-N-E-M-A-N. Freda. F-R-E-D-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Silberberg. S-I-L-B-E-R-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [NON-ENGLISH] was my middle name, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth and age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is the 6th of September, 1923. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was born-- you don't want to know where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, your age. Just tell me your age now, at the moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Metz, Moselle, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's M-E-T-Z, is it? Metz?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-E-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Can you tell me something about your early childhood in France, in Metz, and elsewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in Metz, but we actually moved to Sarreguemines, which is 80 kilometers from Metz, where I was brought up with my three brothers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went to school there. And my father actually was a businessman. He was a merchant in watches and clocks. And we lived there till 1939, when we moved into the interior. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were still all at school when war broke out. But I don't know if you want to know more about what happened in '38, because we were actually at the frontier of-- of Germany. Because Sarreguemines, when the [NON-ENGLISH] became voted to be German, we were actually a frontier town. And in 1938, when Hitler moved into Czechoslovakia, we were already afraid that there might-- that war might break out. So we moved into the interior a little bit. But after six weeks, we came back, because there was no war with France. So we had to go back to school, so we moved back. In 1939, when actually, war broke out, the town, being near the Ligne Maginot, was evacuated. The whole of the path was evacuated. People went right across to the Northwest of France. But my father decided we should go not that far. We should go a little bit nearer. We should come-- we should go as far as Blamont, so in case the war wasn't going to last too long, we could go back. So this was 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get into that part, could we just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go back a bit more to your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Back a bit more

speaker: interviewer
text: A bit more detail about your early childhood. You said that you were at home with your three brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the second oldest of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the second oldest. We came-- perhaps you'd like to know. So my father, we were an Orthodox Jewish family. And my mother and father, they were not born in France. They came to France in 1922. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would you like to know a little bit about life in? Yes, it was a small town, but a very nice little town, and we had many friends. Until 1939, we lived rather a peaceful and pleasant life. It was only since 1938, you know, when everything started.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish people were there in Sarreguemines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarreguemines was a small town, as I said. 20,000 inhabitants. There must have been perhaps 100, or 100, 150 families. I don't know. And-- and we really were in a non-Jewish-- we went to a school, an ordinary lycee. And I went to a girl's school, which was next to the lycee. And they were mixed schools. You know, there were no Jewish schools in those days. And our religion tradition was really once a week. The priests came, the rabbi came, and the [NON-ENGLISH] came from the Catholic church. And they saw we had to read, in the religious tuition.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in school, or after--

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, in school. Because the church, religious education is taught in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there anything like [NON-ENGLISH] that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had private tuition, and we had also, on Sunday, we had to go to [NON-ENGLISH]. My father, being a very religious man, he-- we didn't frequent the ordinary synagogue, because there was a choir and organ. So we had a small, little stebel, a prayer house, where we went. And [INAUDIBLE] was a little bit-- you had Jews that were born and bred for hundreds and hundreds of years in [NON-ENGLISH], and you had the Jews that came from Poland, or Germany, and so on. And somehow, they didn't mix that well. They mixed not that well. So they all kept each other. But for the people that came from Eastern Europe, they were not religious enough for them, you know, and so on. And the others, for the others, they were not born and bred from hundreds of years in [NON-ENGLISH]. But on the whole, they got on all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you belonged to the stebel. Was your father actively involved in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Involved. Yes, yes. He and another man, [NON-ENGLISH]. The two found the place, made the place available, also prayed. Gave the service. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were like the-- he was like the rabbi in a way--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, because my father was educated at the [NON-ENGLISH] Yeshiva, and he was very educated, yes. And very learned. He was-- in fact, he kept his-- he was very strict with himself till the very end. He went to synagogue even in [NON-ENGLISH], when it was very dangerous. But he still went to synagogue till the last.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were many of your friends Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Most of them were Jewish, but I had also non-Jewish friends. But when you were free, when you didn't go to school, you mixed mostly with your Jewish friends. But in school, we were-- it was a non-Jewish school, so we had friends, non-Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of things did you enjoy doing as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I enjoy? We had always a lot of homework, you know. And I liked-- I think I liked swimming already then. I liked swimming, I liked walking, I liked the countryside. Because you mustn't forget, we were not far from the Vorges region. So we went-- Sundays, we went for excursions and picnics and things like that. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you remember particularly about those days in Sarreguemines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I missed Sarreguemines an awful lot when we evacuated. It was like I had lost my roots. And I found that very hard. And I dreamt a lot about it. I missed the company of my school friends. And the life you had, it isn't like today, where young people go out a lot and things like that. It was very much a home life. And girls of a very religious background were kept at home, even the boys. So that-- and I was only 15, 16, you mustn't forget. But the treat that we had was to go to a picture or go to a lovely tea house and get a lovely chocolate and a pastry, and things like that. It wasn't a city like London or Paris, where you had theaters and operas and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in an apartment. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we didn't live in a big house. My father did make a living, but we weren't what we would call very rich Jews. No. He was very-- he concentrated mostly on his children. They should have a good education, and he was prepared to spend a lot of money for education, because you had to pay for your education in those days. For the lycee. You didn't have to pay for the ecole communale, which belonged to the state, but you had to pay for the lycee. You had to pay for my school. You had to pay for your books and everything and tuition and everything. So he provided, and he made sure we had all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked my school, yes. We were quite well-- no. We were quite happy there. Happy. We-- we had to go to school on a Saturday, too. It was-- in France, we had to go to school on Saturday. The free day was Thursday. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't say we-- we didn't-- we didn't really dislike school. The only thing we disliked is that we had to bring back good reports, and they weren't always that good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with going to school on a Saturday as an orthodox Jewish girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to. There was no way out. You had to go to school. You were-- if you were not allowed to write, you could-- so you didn't write. We missed sports that way. Can you see? Because Saturday afternoon was sport or art, and we were just sitting there. But we had to be present. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we accepted it. That's how it was. Like, you couldn't do it differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brothers also go to a school where they had to go on Saturdays, too? The same?

speaker: interviewee
text: The lycee? Yes, of course. Yes, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a mixed-- it was a mixed school that you were at?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a French school. Yeah, the Lycee [FRENCH]. They were French schools, and you were to attend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like being one girl amongst-- with three brothers and being the second oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose it was probably a privileged situation, because being the only girl. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't find I was so much privileged, but I think my brothers thought so. I probably was a little bit more spoiled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you describe yourself as a child and teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terribly protected, very shy, and also really still a little girl, even though I was 15 or 16. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a lesson you learned very quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was going to say something. It's gone from my mind. Did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sarreguemines itself, I think my father was more aware that there were some right-wing movements and things like that. You didn't feel it in school at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we were very few Jewish girls in school. And we just got on with-- with our little circle. And I personally, the non-Jewish friends, I had no-- I had no trouble in those days with anti-Semitism directly in school. No, not in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: But out of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also not. Because we didn't-- we didn't really go out with non-Jewish people, see? Or we were accepted. If we went to a non-Jewish girl's home for homework or saw them after school, there-- there was no problem. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't-- I cannot recall that I had any problems in those days, with my friends, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we move on to '38 and '39--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else about the earlier childhood that you would like to recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seems such a long way away now, especially Sarreguemines. I can only say that I missed it terribly. I missed all of my friends. And after the war, when I went back, because I had to get back to see it again, it seemed such a small, little town. I had outgrown it completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was pleased to see the people that had still returned. Not many. But the ones that had returned, I was pleased to see them again. But everything was different. You know? It was all in my imagination. It had all-- I had outgrown it all. That's all I could say about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even where I was born in Metz, we lived there till I was eight years old. I went back, and I only remember that it was a house with a very big entrance. And to my surprise, when I went back, the entrance had shrunk. The house had shrunk, and everything had shrunk. So that's-- where you see it as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In '39, we moved with our car. We took a few things this time. Because my mother was always afraid of a war, because she went through the 14-18 War. And she was always afraid, and she was terribly afraid already in '38. And she-- we followed the news, you know, coming out of Germany from early on. From '33, we followed it. And my mother was very, very concerned and very worried. And in '39, when we did go into the interior to Blamont, which was about 200 kilometers or miles from Sarreguemines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She was very concerned. And on the third of September, we arrived there just three days before, four days before. When war broke out, it was a very bleak-- dark day. It was really-- we felt terrible about it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had rented a little home, an apartment, where an old lady and her daughter kept it. Had that house, and we had one floor. And we were now refugees. And instead of continuing with studies-- everything had stopped. I-- my parents found a job for me in an office, and my brothers studied from home. They didn't go to school. They studied from home, correspondence. And I was very lucky. I found a job at a company whose-- the first job they gave me was bookkeeping, because I had studied also commercial at the school. They gave me bookkeeping, and I was very bored, so they gave me another job with a director who had polio. And he was very kind, and he could see that I was very shy with the telephone and very shy with doing certain things. And he encouraged me. And I was very lucky. For six months, I worked for him, until we had to run again. And-- and my brothers just studied. And we just lived there as well as we could. But you mustn't forget that as soon as the war broke out, the Germans didn't need to go to fight the Ligne Maginot. They went around it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Therefore, the [NON-ENGLISH] was very soon. We lived in that apartment, and we saw the soldiers, in the winter of 1940, coming down with horse and cart, you know. And it was a terrible sight, thinking that the Germans-- you know, with their strong army, you know? And our French Army was absolutely not ready for such a war. So we stayed there in Blamont and watched the events till June 1940. You managed to live some kind of life there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was full of people from our town, too. There were some French Army personnel in the town. But in those days, you still found food, and you could still live more or less and find a way to get on with life. But it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's right. My father tried to-- he had public reports on merchandise, you know. So he tried to sell some of it so that money was coming in. And so all what we had to do is really, we could only eat and wait for the events, thinking that maybe we'd soon-- life would go back to normal. But of course it didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in-- in-- we just lived through the winter there and followed the events. And by June 1940, of course, the whole-- the Germans invaded France. And we were like everybody else. We-- we were on the running.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a car, but of course, there was no petrol. The car wouldn't go anyway. So we had to-- from that company where I was working, they were willing to put us, a whole lot of people, into one of their vans. [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were on the road like everybody else, with millions of others, some in cars, some on bikes, some with prams with their goods in it. Everybody was on the march. But you mustn't forget that the Italians were also our enemies, and therefore, they were bombarding us on the way down. So we-- we were running. We were-- from the beginning of June, we were running down, and the Germans were following us. They were behind us, the army. And we reached-- by the time we reached-- that was [NON-ENGLISH], the Germans had caught up with us. So we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were literally right behind you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were behind us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were being bombarded. We had sometimes to run from the lorry, and run into the forest, the nearest place where we could hide, and then go back again. And by the time we reached that little village, the Germans had-- had reached our-- our place and were going forward towards Vichy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the commandant of the-- in that village said that all men aged from 18 to 45 should go to Vichy to be recruited. So the people-- I was very ill at the time. I had caught tonsillitis. And-- but we had to go on to Vichy. And when we arrived in Vichy, somehow nobody found the place where you're supposed to be recruited, so we just watched what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the Germans had reached, now, Vichy. They were emptying the shops. You know, all the lovely-- the goods that they could find, either they took it or they paid for it, I don't know. But they were-- they were buying, or they were looting, I don't know. And we could see that wasn't the place to stay, because they were all in Vichy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father decided that we should really continue, and we shouldn't stay in Vichy. And therefore, we-- we decided to go to the next town, which was probably also a similar town to the town where we lived, Sarreguemines, because it was a mining town, a working class town, where we probably could do business and work again. So we went on to Saint-Etienne.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, the Germans had, by that time, also gone right from France. And they had looted-- by that time, they emptied the shops immediately. It's only afterwards that there was-- they made a kind of accord with the Vichy government that France was separated, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were actually in free France. And the rest of France was occupied, and we were called France Libre. But of course, we were under the Vichy government.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in June, he brought out-- the Vichy government brought out the new laws against the Jews. Therefore, everybody had to register. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the [NON-ENGLISH], at the police. You know, where they lived, and so on, and so forth. Where they came from. And we were given ration cards. But of course, our ration cards had always the name-- say Jew on it. J. So we were known as-- that we were-- But everybody knew we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was first time you'd been identified as Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We didn't have a yellow star in free France. It was in the rest of France they wore a Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your parents and your brothers still at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. We, at that point, took another apartment. But when we ran from Blamont, we ran so fast that we left all the valuable things behind, because we were in a panic. So whatever my father said we should take, we took the wrong thing. And we only noticed that we were in the track halfway down France that we had really taken nothing. That we were nearly penniless, you know. And all of the things that we could have made money or got money from, we couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived in Saint-Etienne, we had to find somewhere to live that wasn't expensive. And we had, certainly, no money to buy things at the black market. So things were very, very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we-- we had to find some kind of way to live again. How to make a living. So my father found, again, for me-- I was now 16 and 1/2, 17. He again-- he found a job for me. He said, I can't have only students. You have to go and work. I will see what I can do. My brothers went to school. My older brother, David, he was finishing his matriculation, so he finished it. Armand went to school, to they called [NON-ENGLISH], and the younger one went to an ordinary school. So we all had to-- and so we tried to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel that you had to go to work, and that they were able to carry on studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was war. It was war. You felt that you-- perhaps you were hoping all the time that the day would come that it will finish the war, and you could go on with whatever you wanted to do, you know, to study. But somebody had to bring money. It seemed a shame that the one that was doing matriculation should stop. The others were too young to stop. So it was only me that had to go out. So that's why I went to work.
